# Questions in Livestream 22

Live Stream #22: The Catch-22 of Questions

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12</a> | Are Jannes and Jambres in 2 Timothy 3:8 real Egyptian New Kingdom names that could fit Ramesses's magicians? | He said no. He treated them as later Jewish legendary names added to otherwise unnamed biblical figures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:24</a> | What are good resources on zooarchaeology in Egypt, Nubia, and the ancient Near East? | He especially recommended *The Mammals of Ancient Egypt* and suggested pairing it with more focused regional studies such as works on Abydos and the ivory trade. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:34</a> | Was ancient Egypt a kind of free-market economy? | Only partly. He described a barter-based market system inside a heavily constrained redistributive state economy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=1010" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:50</a> | What caused the fallout between Ramesses VIII and the Theban priesthood? | He said the exact cause is unclear, but the broader hostility came from the priesthood's long resentment of the Ramesside line and preference for a Theban dynasty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=1335" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:15</a> | What really happened in Nebuchadnezzar's move into Egypt, is "conquest" the right word, and what resources address it? | He said it was closer to a raid and temple-pillage than a full conquest; he could not name many specific resources beyond a possible mention in Ian Shaw. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=1689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:09</a> | How did Egyptians represent numbers in the time of Ramesses II? | He said Egyptian numerals were decimal, using separate signs for powers of ten and a working system for fractions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=1893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:33</a> | Was there any news about Zahi Hawass's fate? | He said he had not heard anything suggesting Hawass had gone missing and thought Hawass was still influential in Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=2049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:09</a> | What kind of tombs would Jacob and Joseph likely have had in Egypt? | He leaned toward rock-cut tombs on higher ground near Avaris rather than wet low-lying necropolis burials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=2298" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:18</a> | How are his spoken German and spoken Middle Egyptian? | He said his spoken German is not good, his Japanese is better, and Middle Egyptian is mainly taught as a reading language rather than a spoken one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=2620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:40</a> | Can Joshua 17:16 and its "chariots of iron" language help date the conquest to the Late Bronze or early Iron Age? | Yes. He said it fits the overlap period when late Bronze and early Iron material culture meet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=2902" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:22</a> | When will there be a Coptic revivalist movement, and would it be Bohairic or Sahidic? | He said he would love to see a Coptic revivalist movement and praised the resilience of Coptic Christian culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=3053" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:53</a> | What is his favorite anime, and why is it not Cowboy Bebop? | He said he had not yet seen *Cowboy Bebop* and named *Mobile Suit Gundam*, especially *Thunderbolt*, among his favorites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=3174" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:54</a> | Are Genesis 1 and 2 best understood as mythohistory, and are they influenced by Egypt, Ugarit, and Babylonia? | In a qualified sense, yes. He described them as anti-myth or mythohistory that engages many ancient creation myths at once. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=3479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:59</a> | Why does he not take the sons of God in Genesis 6 as divine beings mingling with women? | He pointed to priestly-title alternatives, ancient Near Eastern vow-breaking motifs, and theological and biological problems with angel-human offspring. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=3915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:15</a> | What books would he recommend for teenagers starting apologetics? | He said to start with logic and church history rather than flashy apologetics titles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=4087" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:07</a> | Why were Psusennes I's remains just skeletal? | He said the tomb at Tanis flooded; water destroys mummified tissue and leaves mostly bones. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=4172" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:32</a> | What does he think happened to the Ark of the Covenant? | He favored the Talmudic option that it was taken to Babylon and melted down rather than hidden under the Temple Mount. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=4353" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:33</a> | What does he think of people who say the Bible is poorly written and inconsistent, and that Ramesses cannot be the pharaoh of the Exodus? | He rejected the poor-writing claim, blamed many problems on translation or poor reading, and said the Ramesses and Akhenaten objections were not strong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=4689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:09</a> | If early prophets did not speak against Asherah, does that mean they endorsed her? | No. He said silence is not endorsement and argued that some key evidence is often misread, especially the "his asherah" inscription. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=4928" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:08</a> | What are his opinions on Logos, and does he know any secret discounts? | He said he does not use Logos much, had heard it was good, and did not know of secret discounts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=5021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:41</a> | Are the seraphim basically dragons from various world mythologies? | He said no. He described them more narrowly as winged serpentine fire beings, not general dragon gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=5342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:02</a> | What is his favorite geography book on the ancient Near East? | He recommended Barry Beitzel's *The New Moody Atlas of the Bible*. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=5581" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:01</a> | What does he think of Tel el-Hammam as Sodom and of the Shroud of Turin? | He was skeptical of both, calling the Sodom case especially weak and poorly supported. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=5898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:18</a> | What are his thoughts on *Dune* 2021? | He was looking forward to it and thought the production appeared to capture the scale and strangeness needed for *Dune*. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=6033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:33</a> | Could major Egyptian gods such as Ra, Horus, or Osiris originally have been human rulers? | Possibly. He said Egyptian tradition sometimes treats gods as earlier kings, and deification of humans did happen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/o5hu9f9P6Do?t=6360" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:00</a> | Did the Egyptians have flood myths, and how do they differ from or correspond to the biblical flood and other ancient Near Eastern flood stories? | Not in the same way Mesopotamia did. He said the annual Nile flood reduced the need for a single-catastrophe myth, while Egyptian water, creation, and temple-box ritual motifs still provide useful parallels. |
